principles.label: Hardware-Algorithm Co-design
principles.summary: Hardware and algorithms must be co-designed to maximize performance and efficiency.
principles.details: By understanding hardware constraints and opportunities, algorithm designers can make trade-offs (like using lower precision or quantization) that drastically increase throughput. This mutually-reinforcing loop is central to advancing AI capabilities.
principles.representative_quote: yes, quantization is irritating, but your model is going to be three times faster, so you're going to have to deal.

principles.label: Consolidate AI Research and Compute
principles.summary: Stop fragmenting compute and ideas across multiple siloed teams; combining efforts into a single, unified model maximizes compute investment and accelerates progress.
principles.details: Separate research efforts lead to fragmented work and duplicated use of compute resources. Merging teams aligns agendas and optimizes resource allocation to build state-of-the-art multimodal models.
principles.representative_quote: instead of fragmenting our compute and our ideas let's all work together and build a single kind of model that is multimmodal that uh has you know a lot comput investment behind a more singular effort

principles.label: AI for Societal Benefit
principles.summary: Focus AI applications on solving problems that help billions of people in societally beneficial ways.
principles.details: Machine learning can distribute the wisdom of experts globally, packaging gold-standard expertise into a model and deploying it to regions suffering from severe shortages of trained professionals (e.g., healthcare).
principles.representative_quote: we can use machine learning to bring the wisdom of experts through a machine learning model anywhere in the world and that's really a huge huge opportunity

principles.label: Chain of Thought as Inference Compute
principles.summary: Chain of thought prompting is a way of allocating more compute at inference time to improve reasoning.
principles.details: Because every generated token requires a pass through the model, prompting a model to show its work step-by-step effectively gives it more computational time to arrive at the correct answer.
principles.representative_quote: you're actually giving the model more compute to think at inference time, right? Because every token it needs to generate is a pass through the model.

principles.label: Collaborative Innovation
principles.summary: Breakthroughs come from combining 'in the air' ideas and partnering with people who possess complementary expertise.
principles.details: Innovation rarely happens in a vacuum. It involves drawing inspiration from existing, separate research ideas and collaborating with experts in different domains to solve the unsolved aspects of a new problem.
principles.representative_quote: a really good way to do interesting things is to partner with people who know things that you don't.

principles.label: Frontier Models and Distillation Synergy
principles.summary: Both massive frontier models and smaller distilled models are necessary; frontier models push boundaries, while distillation uses their rich learning signals to create affordable, low-latency models.
principles.details: Using a powerful teacher model's probability distribution over possible answers gives the student model nuanced information about relationships between concepts, rather than just a binary correct/incorrect signal.

principles.label: Latency as a First-Class Objective
principles.summary: Low latency is a prerequisite for the future of complex, agentic AI workflows.
principles.details: As users ask models to perform increasingly complex, multi-step tasks (like writing an entire software package), the model will need to generate vastly more tokens. Low latency is required to make these workflows practical.
principles.representative_quote: I think latency is actually a pretty important characteristic for these models because we’re going to want models to do much more complicated things that are going to involve, you know, generating many more tokens...

principles.label: Massively Multi-task Models
principles.summary: Build single, multi-task models rather than separate models for every individual problem.
principles.details: Instead of training a model from scratch for every new problem, a single giant model that already knows how to do 10,000 things can learn the 10,001st thing with vastly fewer examples by building on its existing representations.
principles.representative_quote: we tend to Train separate models for each different problem we care about I think this is a bit misguided like really we want one model that does a lot of things so that it can build on the knowledge in how it does thousands or millions of different things

principles.label: Sparsely Activated Models
principles.summary: Build sparsely activated models to balance massive capacity with inference efficiency.
principles.details: You want models to have massive capacity to remember things, but you need them to be highly efficient during inference by only activating a small percentage of the network per token.
principles.representative_quote: you'd like you know trillions of parameters but activate only you know one 1% or 5% or 10% of that

principles.label: The Pareto Frontier of Distillation
principles.summary: Push the frontier of capabilities with massive models, then distill those capabilities into affordable, low-latency models.
principles.details: Frontier models reveal new capabilities and solve complex reasoning tasks, but smaller models are necessary for widespread, low-latency, and agentic use cases. You must have the massive frontier model first in order to distill its knowledge into a highly capable smaller model.

principles.label: Unified Multimodal Models
principles.summary: General multimodal models tend to dominate vertical silos and should be exposed to non-human modalities.
principles.details: Unified models outperform specialized, symbolic systems by leveraging shared representations across different modalities. Including data like LIDAR or genomics teaches the model that these modalities exist and have meaning in the world.
principles.representative_quote: I think it’s also really useful to have Gemini know about non-human modalities. Like LIDAR sensor data from say, Waymo vehicles or like robots...

principles.label: Design Systems for 5-10x Scale
principles.summary: Design systems to scale by factors of 5 or 10, but not 100x, because massive scale usually enables a completely different and more efficient point in the design space.
principles.details: If you design a system for X and something suddenly becomes 100X, the new scale will require and enable a completely new architecture that wouldn't make sense at the original scale.
principles.representative_quote: you're want to design a system so that the most important characteristics could scale by like factors of five or 10 but probably not beyond that because often what happens is if you design a system for X and something suddenly becomes 100X that would enable a very different point in the design space

principles.label: Evaluate Small-Scale Research by Slope
principles.summary: When compute is limited, evaluate the trend (slope) of performance across extremely small scales rather than its absolute performance against the current state-of-the-art.
principles.details: This prevents researchers from abandoning truly unique and interesting ideas just because they don't immediately beat heavily optimized baselines.
principles.representative_quote: If the slope, if it's below the baseline, but the slope looks good, that's a really interesting idea.

principles.label: General Models Beat Specialized Ones
principles.summary: General, unified models will win out over specialized, discrete symbolic systems in most cases.
principles.details: Emulating the brain's distributed representation is more effective than building completely separate, discrete symbolic systems for different tasks.
principles.representative_quote: general models will win out over specialized ones in most cases

principles.label: Generative AI Utility vs Factuality
principles.summary: Generative AI models are highly useful for creative and summarization tasks even when they aren't perfectly factual.
principles.details: While exact factuality is crucial for search queries, generative models excel at tasks like summarization, coding, and creative writing where strict factual accuracy is less critical.
principles.representative_quote: It’s a hard problem and even though these models are not factual all the time – they are still useful.

principles.label: Hardware-Software Co-design
principles.summary: Hardware and software must be co-designed based on where the ML research puck is going 2-6 years out.
principles.details: Hardware takes years to design and deploy, so hardware teams need ML researchers to predict what computations will be important, while ML researchers must adapt architectures to run efficiently on upcoming chips.
principles.representative_quote: we really want to take advantage of being able to co-design what should future TPUs look like based on where we think the sort of ML research puck is going

principles.label: Optimize for Experimental Velocity
principles.summary: Reducing experimental turnaround time fundamentally changes the qualitative feel and pace of scientific research.
principles.details: Getting iteration time down from weeks or months to minutes or hours prevents engineers from waiting around and fundamentally improves how they experiment, work, and discover.
principles.representative_quote: because there's a very different qualitative feel to doing science and research in a domain where an experiment takes a month versus doing it in a domain where you know minutes or hours you get you know an answer

principles.label: Push on Latency
principles.summary: Pushing on latency is critical for user experience and system capabilities.
principles.details: Low-latency interactions are much more delightful for users and allow underlying systems to perform complex background tasks (like parallel rollouts) between interactions.
principles.representative_quote: I'm a big believer in pushing on latency because I think being able to have really low latency interactions with a system you're using is just much more delightful than something that is, you know, 10 times as slow

principles.label: Reasoning over Memorization
principles.summary: Devote parameter space to reasoning, not memorization of obscure facts that can easily be retrieved.
principles.details: It is inefficient to use precious model capacity to memorize facts. Models should focus on reasoning and use retrieval as a tool.
principles.representative_quote: having the model devote precious parameter space to remember obscure facts that could be looked up is actually not the best use of that parameter space

principles.label: Scale and Algorithms Multiply
principles.summary: Combining larger scale (compute and data) with better model architectures yields exponential, rather than additive, improvements.
principles.details: When models learn more per floating-point operation, the gains from scale and the gains from algorithms multiply together.
principles.representative_quote: If you can have much larger scale and much better model architectures that are able to learn more, you know, per floating point operation, those things together mean instead of, you know, 20x from scale, you have 20x from scale and 50x from algorithms and you end up with a thousandx better thing

principles.label: Scale by Factors of 5 or 10
principles.summary: Design systems to scale by factors of 5 or 10, but not 100.
principles.details: If a system's load increases by 100x, it usually justifies a completely different architectural design that wouldn't make sense at the current scale. Over-designing for 100x prematurely leads to suboptimal solutions.
principles.representative_quote: I think a good design principle is you’re going to want to design a system so that the most important characteristics could scale by like factors of five or 10, but probably not beyond that...

frameworks.label: Hardware-ML Co-design (Predicting the Puck)
frameworks.summary: A multi-year process for designing AI accelerators by predicting future ML workloads and stripping away general-purpose compute.
frameworks.details: 1. Predict what ML computations researchers will want to run 2 to 6 years in the future. 2. Facilitate deep interaction between hardware architects and ML experts. 3. Strip away general-purpose computing requirements (branchy C++ code, pointers). 4. Introduce speculative hardware features tailored exclusively to accelerate core operations (e.g., low-precision linear algebra).
frameworks.representative_quote: you’re trying to predict two to six years out where, what ML computations will people want to run two to six years out in a very fast changing field.

frameworks.label: Neural Architecture Search (AutoML)
frameworks.summary: Automating the design of neural network architectures using a model-generating model and reinforcement learning.
frameworks.details: 1. Use a model-generating model to generate descriptions of different neural network architectures. 2. Train each candidate for a few hours. 3. Evaluate their performance. 4. Use the accuracy/loss as a reinforcement learning signal to steer the generator toward better architectures.
frameworks.representative_quote: we're going to generate ten model architectures we're going to train each of them for a few hours and then we're going to use the loss of the generative models as a reinforcement learning signal for the model generating model

frameworks.label: 1/1000th Scale Vetting
frameworks.summary: A method for exploring a massive number of new machine learning architectures efficiently.
frameworks.details: 1. Create a 1/1000th scale version of the problem. 2. Vet hundreds of thousands of ideas or variations on that small scale. 3. Identify the approaches that seem promising. 4. Scale up only the successful ideas to medium and then large-scale production training.
frameworks.representative_quote: It's very helpful to have a 1/1000th scale problem and then vet 100,000 ideas on that, and then scale up the ones that seem promising.

frameworks.label: Back-of-the-Envelope System Design
frameworks.summary: A mental iteration process for evaluating large-scale distributed systems before writing code.
frameworks.details: 1. Identify the most important design parameters (QPS, index size). 2. Use fundamental latency and energy numbers (SRAM vs. DRAM, disk seek times) to evaluate bottlenecks. 3. Perform mental thought experiments to test how the design holds up if traffic doubles or triples. 4. Iterate mentally before committing to code.
frameworks.representative_quote: I’m a big fan of thinking through designs in your head, just kind of playing with the design space a little before you actually do a lot of writing of code.

frameworks.label: Deterministic Replay for SDC Detection
frameworks.summary: A method for monitoring distributed ML training and detecting silent data corruption (SDC) in hardware.
frameworks.details: 1. Monitor training metrics for anomalies, such as spikes in the norm of the gradient. 2. Automatically trigger a deterministic replay of the last few training steps. 3. Compare the results: if the answers match, the spike is likely data-driven; if they differ, it indicates hardware silent data corruption. 4. Evict the defective hardware pod.
frameworks.representative_quote: if we see a gradient spike, then we will effectively replay that step or the last few steps and see if we get the same answer. And if we get the same answer, we conclude, well, that's probably okay. That was probably due to the data, not due to hardware errors.

frameworks.label: MapReduce
frameworks.summary: A programming model for processing and generating large data sets with a parallel, distributed algorithm on a cluster.
frameworks.details: 1. Map: Process each input record and emit key-value pairs. 2. Reduce: Aggregate all values for each key. This abstracts away the complexities of distributed computing, machine failures, and data replication.

frameworks.label: Model Distillation
frameworks.summary: A method to morph a large, powerful model into a smaller, cheaper model without losing significant capability.
frameworks.details: 1. Train a highly capable, large teacher model. 2. Run inputs through the teacher model to generate a probability distribution (soft targets) over the possible outputs. 3. Train a smaller student model using these soft targets, which provides a richer gradient signal than raw data. 4. Deploy the smaller model.
frameworks.representative_quote: you can effectively use a powerful teacher model to make a smaller and cheaper student model from the uh you know the more sophisticated predictions that the larger model can make.

frameworks.label: Self-Supervised Pre-training and Fine-tuning
frameworks.summary: A workflow for leveraging massive amounts of unlabeled data when labeled data is scarce.
frameworks.details: 1. Pre-train a model on a massive amount of unlabeled data using a self-supervised objective (e.g., dropping 15% of words and predicting the missing ones). 2. Take the pre-trained model and fine-tune it using the small amount of labeled examples for your specific target task.
frameworks.representative_quote: step one you can pre train a model on lots and lots of text using this fill in the blanks self supervisor objective function and then step two you can then take a language task you really care about... and now use your paltry 10,000 examples

frameworks.label: Speculative Decoding
frameworks.summary: A technique for decoding from very large transformer models to improve inference efficiency and overcome memory bandwidth limits.
frameworks.details: 1. Use a fast, small drafter model to quickly generate a sequence of draft tokens (e.g., 8 tokens). 2. Have the target large model check the draft tokens in parallel. 3. Accept the correct prefix of tokens based on the large model's probability distribution.
frameworks.representative_quote: you have a fast drafter model where you don't need the full sophistication of a much larger model and you can quickly generate the next say eight tokens and then have the target large model check them in parallel.

frameworks.label: System Design Scaling Assessment
frameworks.summary: A framework for designing new systems or evaluating their capacity to handle future growth.
frameworks.details: 1. Identify the most important design parameters (e.g., queries per second, index size). 2. Evaluate how the system will perform if traffic doubles or triples. 3. Design the system to comfortably scale by a factor of 5 or 10. 4. Avoid designing for 100x scale; instead, recognize that 100x scale will require and enable a completely new architecture.

mental_models.label: Sparsely Activated Multi-Task Model (The Brain Analogy)
mental_models.summary: Viewing a machine learning model as a vast network of specialized components where only a tiny percentage of parameters activate per task.
mental_models.details: Instead of a monolithic block, the system searches for a 'pathway' through existing components that already have relevant expertise. This mirrors the efficiency and storage capacity of the human brain, allowing models to scale to trillions of parameters efficiently.
mental_models.representative_quote: I think what we actually want is a model that's very very big like think you know 100 billion trillion parameters but where for any given thing you activate only a tiny fraction of it 1% of it 5% of it you know your brain works this way

mental_models.label: AI Agents as a Managed Team of Interns
mental_models.summary: Shifting the paradigm of AI interaction from a 1:1 chatbot to a human manager coordinating a large, hierarchical team of specialized virtual assistants or interns.
mental_models.details: Instead of interacting with 50 individual agents directly, a developer might manage 5 sub-teams of agents, similar to managing a 50-person human organization.
mental_models.representative_quote: the human coordinating the activities of a dozen or hundred AI agents doing stuff on their behalf where they've probably the human is probably weekly specified what it is they want.

mental_models.label: Energy-Based Cost of Computation
mental_models.summary: Evaluating system bottlenecks in terms of energy (picojoules per bit) rather than just FLOPs, recognizing that data movement is vastly more expensive than computation.
mental_models.details: The actual math (multiplying numbers) is incredibly cheap, but moving the data from memory to the compute unit is orders of magnitude more expensive. This explains why batching is necessary: you must reuse a parameter across a batch of inputs to amortize the massive energy cost of moving it.

mental_models.label: Neural Nets as Scientific Simulators
mental_models.summary: Viewing expensive scientific simulators as 'teachers' that generate training data for a neural net to approximate.
mental_models.details: Running a quantum chemistry or earthquake simulator to generate data, then training a neural net that achieves indistinguishable accuracy hundreds of thousands of times faster.
mental_models.representative_quote: you essentially get indistinguishable accuracy from using the real simulator but it's three hundred thousand times faster

mental_models.label: The Illusion of Infinite Context
mental_models.summary: Using lightweight retrieval funnels to narrow millions of tokens to a small relevant context window, rather than scaling quadratic attention.
mental_models.details: Instead of trying to scale quadratic attention mechanisms to trillions of tokens (which is computationally infeasible), build a funnel system analogous to search ranking to feed only the most relevant documents into the heavy LLM.
mental_models.representative_quote: I think if you could give the illusion that you can attend to trillions of tokens, that would be amazing.

mental_models.label: AI as an Expert Multiplier
mental_models.summary: Viewing AI not as a replacement for human workers, but as a distribution mechanism for highly specialized expertise.
mental_models.details: Using models trained by a consensus of top specialists to screen for diseases in regions where there is a severe shortage of doctors.
mental_models.representative_quote: we can use machine learning to bring the wisdom of experts through a machine learning model anywhere in the world and that's really a huge huge opportunity

mental_models.label: Attending to the Internet
mental_models.summary: The ultimate goal of long-context models is giving the model the illusion of attending to trillions of tokens (the whole internet, all personal state) via a funnel of retrieval and ranking.
mental_models.details: Using highly parallel, lightweight models to filter trillions of tokens down to a few hundred documents, which are finally processed by the most capable frontier model.
mental_models.representative_quote: I think what you would really want is can I attend to the internet while I answer my question right

mental_models.label: Context Window as a "Crisp" Document
mental_models.summary: Viewing the context window not as learned weights, but as handing a human a large document to read directly.
mental_models.details: Training data is "muddled and fuzzy" like a distant memory, while context is "crisp" and immediately accessible, allowing the model to directly summarize and pull pieces from it.
mental_models.representative_quote: The information in the context window of the model the input that you give it is very crisp because you haven't mixed it with anything else.

mental_models.label: Energy-Based Analysis of Computation
mental_models.summary: Viewing the cost of ML operations not just in time, but in the energy (picojoules) required to move data across a chip or from memory.
mental_models.details: Moving data is orders of magnitude more expensive than the math operation itself. This lens explains why batching is necessary: moving a parameter from SRAM to the multiplier costs 1000 picojoules, while the multiply costs 1 picojoule. Batching amortizes the massive energy cost of data movement.
mental_models.representative_quote: Ideally you'd like to use batch size one because the latency would be great but the energy cost and the compute cost inefficiency that you get is quite large.

mental_models.label: Foundational Models as Infrastructure
mental_models.summary: Viewing large language models not as standalone consumer products, but as underlying engines or infrastructure that power a wide ecosystem of different applications.
mental_models.representative_quote: I would think of Gemini as a model that sits underneath lots of group products, rather than necessarily a new product.

mental_models.label: Heuristics as Learning Problems
mental_models.summary: Viewing handwritten heuristics inside traditional computer systems as machine learning problems that should adapt to actual usage patterns.
mental_models.details: Instead of writing static rules in compilers, operating systems, or storage systems, use learned models that automatically recognize and adapt to specific usage contexts.
mental_models.representative_quote: I think there's huge potential for the thousands, and thousands, and thousands of heuristics in computer systems to really mostly be learned and adapt to how they're actually being used.

mental_models.label: Ideas in the Air
mental_models.summary: A lens for viewing research progress: breakthroughs are rarely entirely novel, but rather combinations of floating concepts.
mental_models.details: New breakthroughs usually come from 'squinting at' several existing, separate research ideas and combining them with one missing piece to solve a new problem.
mental_models.representative_quote: I like to think of a lot of ideas as being partially in the air, where there are a few different, maybe separate research ideas that one is squinting at when you’re trying to solve a new problem.

mental_models.label: Prompting as Executive Communication
mental_models.summary: Treating prompt engineering like writing an internal memo to an executive, where words must be weighed carefully and intent must be clearly specified.
mental_models.details: When writing a prompt, you weigh your words very carefully, just as you would when communicating with a high-level executive.

mental_models.label: Sparse Models as the Human Brain
mental_models.summary: Reasoning about neural network activation by comparing it to the human brain, where only specific regions activate for specific tasks rather than the entire brain firing at once.
mental_models.details: Using Mixture of Experts (MoE) to route inputs to specific sub-networks so the model has a massive capacity but keeps inference costs low by only activating a small percentage of parameters per token.
mental_models.representative_quote: That's not how our brains work, right? We have very different pieces of our brains that are useful for different kinds of things.

mental_models.label: Squishy Weights vs. Sharp Context
mental_models.summary: Understanding how LLMs store information: trained parameters are 'squishy' and prone to hallucination, while the context window is 'sharp'.
mental_models.details: When a model relies on its 'squishy' trained parameters for specific facts, it might hallucinate. But information placed directly in the input context window remains 'sharp and clear' because the attention mechanism can pinpoint exact text.

mental_models.label: The Giant Computer Illusion
mental_models.summary: Abstracting away complex, distributed hardware topologies so that researchers can write code as if they are operating a single massive machine.
mental_models.details: Using systems like Pathways to allow a single Python process to drive training across thousands of TPU chips, automatically handling network routing across pods and data centers.

heuristics.label: Bigger Model, More Data
heuristics.summary: Bigger model, more data, better results.
heuristics.details: The mantra that drove Google's neural network scaling efforts for years.
heuristics.representative_quote: Bigger model, more data, better results.

heuristics.label: Consensus Labeling
heuristics.summary: To resolve human labeler disagreement, get each example labeled by many experts and take the consensus.

heuristics.label: Energy Cost of Data Movement
heuristics.summary: Moving data costs 1000x more energy than computing it.

heuristics.label: Low Precision for Energy Savings
heuristics.summary: Use very low precision (7 or 8 bits) for neural network inference to save energy on data transfer.
heuristics.details: Because it's joules per bit transferred, reducing bit precision saves a tremendous amount of energy without significantly impacting inference quality.
heuristics.representative_quote: I'm a big fan of very low precision because I think that gets that saves you a tremendous amount of energy, right? Because it's joules per bit that you're transferring.

heuristics.label: Predict Workloads 2-6 Years Out
heuristics.summary: Predict ML workloads 2-6 years out when co-designing hardware.

heuristics.label: Back-of-the-Envelope First
heuristics.summary: Do back-of-the-envelope calculations before writing code.
heuristics.representative_quote: I’m a big fan of thinking through designs in your head, just kind of playing with the design space a little before you actually do a lot of writing of code.

heuristics.label: Design for 5-10x Scale
heuristics.summary: Design systems to scale by factors of 5 or 10, but probably not beyond that.
heuristics.representative_quote: you're want to design a system so that the most important characteristics could scale by like factors of five or 10 but probably not beyond that

heuristics.label: Diminishing Returns on Benchmarks
heuristics.summary: Benchmarks are most useful when initial scores are 10-30%; once they hit 95%, you get diminishing returns.

heuristics.label: Don't Hype Unfinished Work
heuristics.summary: Don't hype things that aren't done yet.
heuristics.representative_quote: I just don't like to hype things that aren't done yet.

heuristics.label: Evaluate Utility over Perfect Factuality
heuristics.summary: Evaluate an AI model's utility based on the specific task's need for exact factuality.
heuristics.details: Don't block useful features (like creative writing or coding) just because the model isn't perfectly factual for search queries.
heuristics.representative_quote: It’s a hard problem and even though these models are not factual all the time – they are still useful.

heuristics.label: General Guides over Custom Prompts
heuristics.summary: Provide coding agents with general software engineering guides rather than writing custom prompts for every edge case.

heuristics.label: Multiple Rollouts for Hallucination Reduction
heuristics.summary: To decrease hallucinations in critical applications, generate multiple rollouts and have the model assess which ones are correct.

heuristics.label: Partner for Knowledge
heuristics.summary: The way to do interesting things is to partner with people who know things you don't.
heuristics.representative_quote: a really good way to do interesting things is to partner with people who know things that you don't.

heuristics.label: Read 100 Abstracts
heuristics.summary: Read 100 abstracts instead of deeply reading one paper to connect the dots across fields.
heuristics.representative_quote: I think you would be much better served to take the time to read 100 abstracts of 100 different papers and sort of understand the highest level concept that they each have.

heuristics.label: Scale by 10x, Not 100x
heuristics.summary: Design systems to scale by 5x or 10x, but not 100x.
heuristics.representative_quote: I think a good design principle is you’re going to want to design a system so that the most important characteristics could scale by like factors of five or 10, but probably not beyond that...

signature_quotes.label: AI Product Hesitation
signature_quotes.summary: On Google's initial hesitation to release generative AI products.
signature_quotes.details: Reflecting on why Google hesitated and allowed OpenAI to launch the first viral AI product.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: I think we were a little caught up on the fact that these models make mistakes sometimes.

signature_quotes.label: Bigger Model, More Data
signature_quotes.summary: Describing the mantra that drove Google's neural network scaling efforts.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: Bigger model, more data, better results.

signature_quotes.label: Evaluating Small Scale Research
signature_quotes.summary: Giving advice to researchers with limited compute on how to evaluate their ideas.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: If the slope, if it's below the baseline, but the slope looks good, that's a really interesting idea.

signature_quotes.label: Experimental Velocity
signature_quotes.summary: On how iteration speed changes the nature of research.
signature_quotes.details: Discussing the importance of scaling infrastructure to reduce experimental turnaround time.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: there's a very different qualitative feel to doing science and research in a domain where an experiment takes a month versus doing it in a domain where you know minutes or hours you get you know an answer

signature_quotes.label: Fragmenting Compute
signature_quotes.summary: On the origin of the Gemini project and the need to unify efforts.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: I sort of said this is stupid why don't we all work together and have instead of fragmenting our compute and our ideas let's all work together and build a single kind of model

signature_quotes.label: General vs Specialized Models
signature_quotes.summary: Reflecting on the shift from specialized models to unified models.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: General models will win out over specialized ones in most cases.

signature_quotes.label: Growth of Ideas
signature_quotes.summary: On the exponential growth of ML research.
signature_quotes.details: Discussing how the exponential growth of ML research papers is making up for the slowing down of Moore's Law.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: we've replaced computational growth with growth of ideas

signature_quotes.label: Hardware Failures
signature_quotes.summary: Discussing the inevitability of hardware failures and silent data corruption at massive scale.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: Sadly not all chips or machines or network cards or cables work as designed. That's a very sad but true state of the world.

signature_quotes.label: Hardware Specialization
signature_quotes.summary: On the foundational insight behind TPUs.
signature_quotes.details: Explaining the foundational insight behind the design of Google's Tensor Processing Units (TPUs).
signature_quotes.representative_quote: If you can build hardware that is only designed to accelerate low-precision linear algebra, you're golden.

signature_quotes.label: Inference Compute
signature_quotes.summary: Explaining why chain-of-thought prompting improves model performance on reasoning tasks.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: You're actually giving the model more compute to think at inference time, right? Because every token it needs to generate is a pass through the model.

signature_quotes.label: Partnering for Knowledge
signature_quotes.summary: On the best career advice for doing interesting things.
signature_quotes.details: When asked for the best piece of advice he's received or observed in his career.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: a really good way to do interesting things is to partner with people who know things that you don't.

signature_quotes.label: Prompting as Executive Communication
signature_quotes.summary: Comparing prompt engineering to writing an internal memo.

signature_quotes.label: Quantization Trade-offs
signature_quotes.summary: On the necessity of algorithmic trade-offs for hardware speed.
signature_quotes.details: Discussing the trade-offs algorithm designers must make to leverage specialized hardware.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: yes, quantization is irritating, but your model is going to be three times faster, so you're going to have to deal.

signature_quotes.label: Reasoning vs Memorization
signature_quotes.summary: Discussing the tension between model capacity, memorization, and retrieval.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: Having the model devote precious parameter space to remember obscure facts that could be looked up is actually not the best use of that parameter space.

signature_quotes.label: Scaling Systems
signature_quotes.summary: On the limits of forward-designing distributed systems.
signature_quotes.details: Explaining how to design large-scale distributed systems.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: I think a good design principle is you’re going to want to design a system so that the most important characteristics could scale by like factors of five or 10, but probably not beyond that...

signature_quotes.label: Sparse Activation
signature_quotes.summary: On the future architecture of massive neural networks.
signature_quotes.details: Discussing the future architecture of neural networks and moving away from densely activated models.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: I think what we actually want is a model that's very very big like think you know 100 billion trillion parameters but where for any given thing you activate only a tiny fraction of it 1% of it 5% of it you know your brain works this way

signature_quotes.label: System Scaling Limits
signature_quotes.summary: On the principles of system design and scaling.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: You're want to design a system so that the most important characteristics could scale by like factors of five or 10 but probably not beyond that because often what happens is if you design a system for X and something suddenly becomes 100X that would enable a very different point in the design space

anti_patterns.label: Fragmenting AI Research and Compute
anti_patterns.summary: Fragmenting compute and ideas across multiple siloed model teams.
anti_patterns.details: It dilutes compute investment, divides the best people and ideas, and prevents the creation of a powerful, unified, state-of-the-art multimodal model.

anti_patterns.label: Brute-Forcing Long Context
anti_patterns.summary: Relying solely on bigger context windows or naive attention algorithms for trillion-token context.
anti_patterns.details: Naive attention scales quadratically, making it computationally impossible to run on trillions of tokens. True scale requires retrieval systems that give the illusion of infinite context.

anti_patterns.label: Designing for 100x Scale Prematurely
anti_patterns.summary: Designing a system to scale 100x from the start.
anti_patterns.details: A 100x scale usually enables a completely different, more efficient point in the design space that wouldn't make sense at the current scale (e.g., moving from disk-based to in-memory indexes).

anti_patterns.label: Training Isolated, Single-Task Models
anti_patterns.summary: Training a bespoke model to do only one specific thing.
anti_patterns.details: It prevents the model from transferring knowledge to new domains, requires massive amounts of data for every new problem, and blocks the emergence of reasoning capabilities.

anti_patterns.label: Chasing SOTA with Limited Compute
anti_patterns.summary: Chasing state-of-the-art (SOTA) results with limited compute by making incremental tweaks.
anti_patterns.details: It prevents researchers from exploring truly unique and interesting ideas. Small-scale research should focus on the "slope" (trend) of an idea rather than its absolute performance against a baseline.

anti_patterns.label: Custom Distributed Computing Code
anti_patterns.summary: Having engineers write custom distributed computing code for their specific tasks.
anti_patterns.details: Coordinating thousands of machines is extremely difficult because machines fail constantly, data needs to be replicated, and tasks must be re-assigned when workers crash. This leads to a messy codebase.

anti_patterns.label: Deeply Reading Single Papers for General Learning
anti_patterns.summary: Deeply reading a single research paper as a primary method for general learning.
anti_patterns.details: It prevents you from getting a broad overview of many techniques, making it harder to connect the dots across different fields and problems. Reading 100 abstracts is better.

anti_patterns.label: Dense Activation for Every Token
anti_patterns.summary: Activating the entire dense model for every single inference or token.
anti_patterns.details: It is computationally inefficient and limits the total capacity the model can have given a fixed inference budget.

anti_patterns.label: Discrete Symbolic Systems
anti_patterns.summary: Building completely separate discrete symbolic systems alongside neural nets.
anti_patterns.details: It doesn't emulate how real brains intuitively work, which rely on distributed representations rather than discrete symbolic manipulation.

anti_patterns.label: Ignoring Energy Costs at Batch Size 1
anti_patterns.summary: Running inference at batch size 1 without considering energy costs.
anti_patterns.details: While latency is great at batch size 1, the energy cost of moving weights from memory to the compute unit is massive compared to the compute itself. Without batching, you waste massive amounts of energy.

anti_patterns.label: Letting Fear of Mistakes Delay Releases
anti_patterns.summary: Letting the fear of AI models making mistakes prevent or delay the release of AI products.
anti_patterns.details: It allows competitors to capture the market and launch viral products first, causing the cautious company to lose its advantage and be perceived as a laggard.

anti_patterns.label: Monolithic Prompting on Slow Models
anti_patterns.summary: Spending a long time writing a massive, highly-specified prompt for a slow, smart model instead of iterating quickly with a fast model.
anti_patterns.details: Because user intent is often underspecified, a single large prompt might still produce the wrong output, whereas fast, multi-turn iteration allows for quick human alignment and correction.

anti_patterns.label: Over-indexing on Perfect Factuality
anti_patterns.summary: Being overly purist about AI factuality before launching products.
anti_patterns.details: It prevents users from benefiting from the model's capabilities in areas where exact factuality isn't required, such as creative writing, coding, or drafting emails.

anti_patterns.label: Over-optimizing Saturated Benchmarks
anti_patterns.details: Once a benchmark hits ~95%, focusing on it yields diminishing returns and risks leakage or overfitting, rather than building genuinely new capabilities.

anti_patterns.label: Relying on Handwritten Heuristics
anti_patterns.summary: Relying on handwritten heuristics in core computer systems (like OS or compilers).
anti_patterns.details: Handwritten heuristics have to work well in the general case and cannot dynamically adapt to how the system is actually being used in specific contexts. They should be replaced by learned models.
